nativeRange.habitat: Small, low-turbidity headwater streams with gravelly and sandy riffles and pools; also springs and small clear streams with moderate to strong current and high dissolved oxygen

decorPreferences: smooth river gravel or pea-gravel substrate for natural spawning behaviour
rounded cobbles and flat stones as territory markers for males
robust planting (Java fern, Anubias, hornwort, Vallisneria) along tank sides and back
powerhead or spray bar to create a gentle-to-moderate midwater current
open swimming corridor along the front of the tank
tight-fitting lid — males jump during spawning chases

diet.notes: In the wild, the Rainbow Shiner is an opportunistic micro-predator whose diet consists predominantly of aquatic and terrestrial insects — including midge larvae (Chironomidae), adult flies (Diptera), and springtails (Collembola) — with some plant matter also recorded. In the aquarium, the species readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small frozen or live foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, microworms, and tubifex. A varied diet that includes invertebrate-based frozen foods tends to enhance breeding coloration in males. Feed small amounts two to three times daily.

goodWith: other Rainbow Shiners (best kept as a single-species or species-dominant school)
hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon) — share cool-water, high-flow preference
dojo loach (Misgurnus anguillicaudatus) — cool-tolerant bottom dweller
native darters (Etheostoma spp.) in biotope setups
small peaceful gobies with similar water-parameter needs
white cloud mountain minnow — similar cool-water requirement
avoidWith: tropical fish requiring temperatures above 26°C (incompatible thermal range)
fin-nippers such as tiger barbs — males’ colourful finnage is a target
large or predatory fish that can swallow 5–8 cm fish
Red Shiner (Cyprinella lutrensis, fw-fish-217) — different genus, warmer-water preference, hybridisation risk in mixed shiners
ornamental dwarf shrimp — fry and juveniles may be consumed

imageCandidates.notes: H. Zell (Wilhelma) — 5620x2254. Tack-sharp full-profile breeding male showing the diagnostic red fins, violet/electric-blue iridescent head and flanks, and red eye; peak nuptial colour that defines the species.
Przemek Pietrak — 1627x1106. Sharp shoal shot of many individuals, representing the schooling behaviour this species is kept in; warm red-orange flanks with dorsal stripe.
H. Zell — 4706x2254. Second sharp breeding male at a different pose; brilliant red fins, blue-violet iridescence and silvery-pink flanks reinforce the vivid nuptial coloration.

summary: The Rainbow Shiner is a cool-water minnow native to Alabama, Tennessee, and Georgia. Breeding males develop brilliant red, pink, violet, and electric-blue iridescence — often cited as among the most vivid coloration seen in North American freshwater fish. Best kept in groups of eight or more in cool, well-oxygenated water.
careNotes:
The Rainbow Shiner (Notropis chrosomus) is endemic to the Mobile Bay drainage of the southeastern United States — the Coosa, Cahaba, Alabama, and upper Black Warrior river systems spanning northwestern Georgia, Alabama, and southeastern Tennessee. Current ichthyological consensus places the species in the genus Hydrophlox, within the family Leuciscidae (formerly part of Cyprinidae); the older name Notropis chrosomus remains widely used in the aquarium trade. Wild fish occupy low-turbidity headwater streams with gravel-and-cobble riffles, cool temperatures, and high dissolved oxygen; replicating these conditions is the key to success in the aquarium.

Temperature is the single most critical parameter. This species tolerates 4–22°C and actively thrives at the cooler end of that range (14–20°C). It does not require a heater in most temperate homes but must not be combined with tropical species. Warm summer temperatures above 24°C depress immune function and can be fatal; a chiller or basement location may be needed in warm climates. High aeration and water movement via powerhead or spray bar are essential to maintain dissolved oxygen.

Keep in groups of at least 8 — ideally 10 or more. Below this number fish become shy and males rarely display full breeding coloration. A school of 10–15 in a 100–120 L tank with a 90 cm+ footprint is a commonly recommended setup. Gravel or smooth-pebble substrate is preferable to fine sand; the fish are benthopelagic and graze the substrate regularly.

Breeding coloration in males is seasonal and temperature-driven: as water warms toward 16–22°C in late spring (May–June), males develop spectacular metallic red on the snout, rosy-pink and violet flanks, and electric blue-purple on the dorsal surface — earning comparisons to reef fish. Spawning is triggered by slightly warmer water (up to 22°C), increased feeding, and longer photoperiod. Males chase females vigorously; a tight-fitting lid is necessary. Eggs are broadcast over gravel or deposited in Semotilus/Nocomis nests in the wild; in captivity, a fine-gravel or slate-rubble spawning area works. Adults consume eggs, so remove parents or use an egg trap after spawning. Fry hatch in approximately 5–7 days and require infusoria or commercial fry food initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp within a week.

The Rainbow Shiner is sometimes sold under its older accepted name Notropis chrosomus; both names refer to the same species and are widely understood in the hobby. It is unrelated to the Red Shiner (Cyprinella lutrensis, [fw-fish-217]), a different genus from warmer, more turbid waters with less dramatic coloration.
breedingNotes: The Rainbow Shiner is an egg-scattering gravel spawner; wild spawning occurs May–June in water temperatures of 16–25°C. Males in breeding condition develop intense red, pink, violet, and metallic blue coloration and actively court females with chasing and nudging displays. Eggs are broadcast over gravel; no parental care is provided and adults will consume eggs if not separated. To breed in captivity: condition with varied live and frozen foods, allow a cool-winter period at 10–14°C, then raise gradually to 18–22°C in spring with increased feeding and a 14-hour photoperiod to simulate seasonal cues. Provide a dedicated spawning tank (40–60 L) with 3–5 cm of smooth pea-gravel or spawning slate. Remove adults promptly after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 5–7 days at 20°C; fry are free-swimming within a few days of hatching. Feed infusoria or commercial micro-fry food for the first week, then newly hatched brine shrimp. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 12 months.

primary.name: FishBase — Hydrophlox chrosomus (= Notropis chrosomus)
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and morphological reference. Confirmed: family Leuciscidae, order Cypriniformes, max size 8.1 cm TL, common size 5.0 cm TL, native range (Coosa, Cahaba, Alabama, upper Black Warrior river systems, Mobile Bay drainage; NW Georgia, Alabama, SE Tennessee), habitat (gravelly riffles and pools, springs, small clear streams), benthopelagic freshwater. FishBase now lists the accepted name as Hydrophlox chrosomus; Notropis chrosomus is a synonym.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rainbow Shiner (Notropsis chrosomus)
additional.notes: UK trade and husbandry source. Confirmed temperature 10–22°C, pH 6.8–7.8, hardness up to 25°dH, peaceful shoaling species, diet (flake, granules, frozen mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp), spawning in late spring/summer, male colour change, gravel-spawning behaviour, eggs hatch ~1 week, parents eat eggs. Care level: moderate to advanced.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Rainbow Shiner (Notropsis chrosomus), Tank-Bred
additional.notes: US trade/hobbyist source for tank-bred specimens. Temperature 50–72°F (10–22°C), pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 2–15 dKH, min tank 15 gal, adult size 2–3 in (5–7.5 cm), lifespan 3–5 years. Diet: high-quality flake, pellets, Artemia, microworms, Tubifex, Calanus, cyclops plus spirulina. Schooling minimum 6–8, prefers 10+. Peaceful; top/mid water column.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rainbow shiner
additional.notes: Cross-reference for taxonomy, native range, and spawning ecology. Confirmed Hydrophlox chrosomus reclassification, Mobile Bay drainage range, habitat in low-turbidity headwater streams, opportunistic insectivore diet (Chironomidae, Diptera, Collembola), spawning May–June at 16–25°C in Semotilus/Nocomis nests, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Hydrophlox chrosomus (= Notropis chrosomus)
additional.notes: German wholesale hobbyist reference. Confirmed safe temperature range 10–25°C (optimal 18–22°C), peaceful community fish, shoal of 10+ required to prevent shyness, max length ~8 cm, gravel spawner, no parental care, males identified by higher proportion of blue in colouring. Accepts all ornamental fish food of suitable size.

fish.breedingNotes: The Rainbow Shiner is an egg-scattering gravel spawner. Breeding requires cool-winter conditioning at 10–14°C followed by a gradual spring warm-up to 18–22°C, increased feeding, and an extended photoperiod to trigger spawning. Males display brilliant red, violet, and blue breeding coloration during courtship. Parents should be removed promptly after spawning to protect the eggs, which hatch in approximately 5–7 days at 20°C.
